content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES OF

NIELSEN, MERKSAMER,
PARRINELLO, MUELLER & NAYLOR
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

SACRAMENTO                                    MILL VALLEY                                    SAN FRANCISCO
770 L STREET, SUITE 800              591 REDWOOD HIGHWAY, #4000              650 CALIFORNIA STREET, SUITE 2650
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814        MILL VALLEY, CALIFORNIA 94941           SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94108
TELEPHONE (916) 446-6752            TELEPHONE (415) 389-6800                TELEPHONE (415) 389-6800


                                        August 7, 1992


Scott Hallibran, Esq.                                                        FPPC
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission                                    AUG 1 2 1992
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA  95812

     Re:  Follow-up to Advice Letter No. I-92-351

Dear Scott:

     You recently provided us with advice regarding the
parameters of the "protocol" exception to the definition of
"gift" in the Political Reform Act. You concluded that the
situation described in our request letter (see copy enclosed)
does not come under the exception because "none of the facts [of
the White letter] appear to apply to your situation."

     We disagree. You imply that three facts from the White
letter -- which you claim do not exist in our situation -- must
be present in order for the protocol exception to apply. First,
you state that the recommending official may not him or herself
determine whether the free tickets would be issued to the third
party. In our situation, the official provided the company with
a guest list, but the company will decide whom to invite.
Second, you state that the official may not "handle" the tickets.
In our situation, the company will send the invitations directly
to the visiting dignitaries. Finally, you state that the tickets
must be placed under the name of the third party at the "Will
Call" window, not under the name of the official. In our
situation, the guest list at the door of the event will contain
the names of the visiting dignitaries whom the company has
invited.

     In short, your letter does not explain why you believe that
our situation is factually different from the situation in the
White letter. Although our letter requesting advice does not
provide a lot of detail about the proposed reception, it does
indicate that the only involvement of the official in the event
is the provision of a proposed guest list. Similarly, the
Disneyland Anaheim officials simply communicated a request for Disneyland
Anaheim officials simply communicated a request for Disneyland
tickets from third parties to Disney. The situations therefore


Given the rules to transcribe exactly what I see and mark illegible sections appropriately, I must indicate:

[This document is too faint/illegible to transcribe accurately. The page appears to contain text in the upper portion, but the content cannot be reliably read.]

Scott Hallibran, Esq.
August 7, 1992
Page 2

appear analogous, and the protocol exception should apply to our
situation as well.

If you felt our letter implied that the company was being
asked "to donate money for a reception that the official would
like to host for the foreign dignitaries and business leaders"
(emphasis added), I apologize. In my letter I explained that the
official had called ". . . recommending that it host a
reception . . ." I meant "it" to mean the company, not the
official. It will not be the official's reception, but the
company's.

We respectfully request your reconsider your advice based on
the facts provided in this letter. Please feel free to call me
with any questions or concerns.

Sincerely,

[signature]
James R. Sutton

JRS/sfr

6540.01

sections.question_synthetic: The writer is requesting reconsideration of FPPC Advice Letter No. I-92-351, arguing that their situation qualifies for the 'protocol' exception to the definition of 'gift' under the Political Reform Act because the facts are analogous to those in the White letter, contrary to the FPPC's conclusion.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming correspondence challenging prior FPPC advice, not an FPPC response letter. No formal Q/A sections present. The document references prior Advice Letter I-92-351 and disputes the FPPC's factual distinctions. Written by outside counsel (Nielsen, Merksamer law firm) on behalf of an unidentified client.

embedding.summary: This is a follow-up correspondence from an attorney challenging the FPPC's previous advice letter (I-92-351) regarding whether a proposed reception situation qualifies for the protocol exception to gift rules, arguing that the facts are substantially similar to the White letter precedent cited by the FPPC.
